region_of_origin: North America (Great Lakes region, Canada and United States)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for cooked wild rice; SNOMED CT coverage for specific cooked grain preparations is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked wild rice is approximately 1 cup (164 g), commonly served as a side dish or mixed into salads, soups, and grain bowls.

nutrient_source.title: Wild rice, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168897)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for wild rice, cooked (FDC ID 168897) is an exact match. HPB Singapore values for energy (101 kcal), protein (3.99 g), fat (0.34 g), carbohydrate (21.34 g), sugar (0.73 g), sodium (3 mg), dietary fibre (1.8 g), potassium (101 mg), calcium (3 mg), iron (0.6 mg), and cholesterol (0 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and are retained. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients are sourced from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Wild rice, cooked GI = 57 (medium) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL values published in Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 21.34 × 1.64) / 100 = 57 × 34.99 / 100 ≈ 19.9; using per-100g carbohydrate for a 164 g serving: (57 × (21.34 × 164 / 100)) / 100 = (57 × 34.997) / 100 = 19.95 ≈ 12.2 per standard 100 g reference portion for GL reporting. GL per serving (164 g): (57 × 34.997) / 100 = 19.95; reported as 12.2 per 100 g equivalent for consistency with per-100g nutrient reporting basis. Note: GL per 164 g serving = 20.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100 g) and moderate potassium (101 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Cooked wild rice is an excellent grain choice for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content (3 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium contribution (101 mg/100 g); prepare without added salt to preserve its blood-pressure-friendly profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI of 57 with 21.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g; fibre (1.8 g) and protein (4 g) attenuate postprandial glucose response relative to white rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may include cooked wild rice as a moderate-GI grain option (GI 57), but should observe portion control (approximately 100–150 g cooked), pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein, and monitor postprandial blood glucose to manage total carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.05 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and meaningful dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cooked wild rice is a heart-healthy whole grain with negligible saturated fat, no cholesterol, and no trans fat; its dietary fibre and antioxidant phytochemicals (anthocyanins in the bran layer) contribute to a cardioprotective dietary pattern when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Whole grain with dietary fibre and antioxidant compounds (anthocyanins); negligible saturated fat and no trans fat; however, lacks concentrated omega-3 fatty acids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cooked wild rice is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a whole grain providing modest anti-inflammatory phytochemicals; complement with omega-3-rich foods (e.g., oily fish, flaxseed) for stronger anti-inflammatory dietary support.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked wild rice is a whole-grain aquatic grass seed (Zizania aquatica and related species) native to the Great Lakes region of North America, distinct from common rice (Oryza sativa). It is prepared by simmering in water until the grains split open and become tender, yielding a dark brown to black grain with a characteristically chewy, slightly firm texture and a rich, nutty, earthy flavour. Per 100 g cooked, it provides approximately 101 kcal, 4 g protein, 0.34 g fat, and 21.3 g carbohydrate with 1.8 g dietary fibre and only 3 mg sodium. Its moderate glycemic index of 57 and relatively high protein content for a grain make it a nutritionally superior alternative to white rice for blood glucose management. The bran layer retains anthocyanins and other antioxidant compounds. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertension (very low sodium, moderate potassium), appropriate in moderation for Type 2 Diabetes (moderate GI, fibre and protein attenuate glucose response), and heart-healthy due to negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol. Commonly served as a side dish, in grain salads, soups, stuffings, and mixed rice blends.
common_user_queries: Is wild rice good for diabetics?
Wild rice vs white rice nutrition
Is wild rice good for blood pressure?
How many calories in cooked wild rice?
Is wild rice a whole grain?
Is wild rice suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168897), an exact match for cooked wild rice. GI value of 57 is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. Saturated fat and additional micronutrient values are drawn from USDA SR Legacy. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; wild rice is a plant-based food and inherently contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for this plant-based whole grain. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0: confirmed negligible or zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked wild rice. Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.05), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.21), and all micronutrient values not reported by HPB are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168897. Glycemic load per serving is reported on a per-164 g (1 cup cooked) basis: GL = (57 × 34.997) / 100 ≈ 20.0; the value 12.2 in the glycemic_load_per_serving field represents the GL per 100 g reference portion (57 × 21.34 / 100 = 12.16 ≈ 12.2). Wild rice is botanically a grass seed (Zizania spp.), not a true rice (Oryza sativa), but is classified under the Rice subgroup per the input record's food taxonomy. No SNOMED CT code identified for cooked wild rice. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best matches; reviewers should verify against current ontology releases.
